sourceTitle: How to build a wiki for your product team

questions.question: Why does the article describe a product wiki as an "investment in your product"?

options.text: Because it costs money to host a wiki.
options.explanation: The article focuses on the strategic value, not the monetary cost.

options.text: Because it centralizes documentation, reducing disorganization and improving product quality.
options.explanation: The wiki consolidates guides, feedback, and ownership info, preventing scattered knowledge and boosting execution.

options.text: Because it replaces the need for any other tools in the company.
options.explanation: The wiki complements other tools; it doesn't replace every other system.

options.text: Because it eliminates the need for product managers.
options.explanation: Product managers still play a crucial role; the wiki supports them.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a recommended way to create a new page in Notion according to the guide?

options.text: Hover over the sidebar and click the + sign next to WORKSPACE.
options.explanation: This is one of the two methods described.

options.text: Press the / key and type "page" while inside an existing page.
options.explanation: This slash command creates a new page.

options.text: Right‑click any existing page and select "Duplicate".
options.explanation: Duplicating a page is not mentioned as a primary method for creating a fresh wiki page.

options.text: Use the slash command "/page" and press Enter.
options.explanation: This is precisely the slash‑command method described.

questions.question: What is the purpose of using headers (H1, H2, H3) in a product wiki?

options.text: To change the font color of the text.
options.explanation: Headers affect hierarchy, not text color.

options.text: To create a visual hierarchy that makes sections easier to scan and organize.
options.explanation: Headers establish structure, helping users locate information quickly.

options.text: To automatically generate a table of contents on the page.
options.explanation: While Notion can generate a TOC, the guide emphasizes hierarchy, not auto‑generation.

options.text: To enable comments on each section.
options.explanation: Comments can be added anywhere, independent of header level.

questions.question: Which feature allows you to view the same dataset as both a table and a board in Notion?

options.text: Creating a separate database for each view.
options.explanation: Separate databases duplicate data; the guide shows switching views within one database.

options.text: Adding a new view and selecting "Board" from the view options.
options.explanation: You can add a Board view to an existing Table database, letting you toggle between formats.

options.text: Using the "Turn Into" command on a page.
options.explanation: "Turn Into" changes a block type, not database view.

options.text: Embedding a Google Sheet and switching its view mode.
options.explanation: Embedding external sheets is possible but not the method described for native Notion views.

questions.question: When building a user research database, which property type would you choose to allow selecting a single tag from a predefined list (e.g., "Task")?

options.text: Text
options.explanation: Text allows free‑form entry, not controlled tags.

options.text: Single select
options.explanation: Single select lets you define a menu of tags and choose one per row.

options.text: Multi‑select
options.explanation: Multi‑select permits multiple tags, which is not the described single‑tag use case.

options.text: Relation
options.explanation: Relation links to another database, not a simple tag list.

questions.question: How can you quickly create multiple sub‑pages at once inside a wiki?

options.text: Copy‑paste a list of page titles and then click the "Turn Into" → "Page" option.
options.explanation: The guide explains typing each title on its own line, highlighting them, then using Turn Into > Page.

options.text: Select "Add multiple pages" from the sidebar menu.
options.explanation: No such dedicated menu is mentioned.

options.text: Use the shortcut Cmd/Ctrl + N repeatedly.
options.explanation: This creates single pages, not batch creation.

options.text: Import a CSV file with page titles.
options.explanation: CSV import is not described as a quick method for sub‑pages.

questions.question: Which of the following best explains how comments improve remote collaboration on a product wiki?

options.text: Comments automatically change the page layout to a chat view.
options.explanation: Comments appear alongside content; they don’t alter layout.

options.text: They allow asynchronous discussions, keep ideas visible, and let teammates tag each other for quick follow‑up.
options.explanation: The guide highlights tagging, asynchronous conversation, and visibility as collaboration benefits.

options.text: Comments replace the need for version history.
options.explanation: Version history is a separate feature; comments complement it.

options.text: They automatically assign tasks to the person mentioned.
options.explanation: Mentioning notifies but doesn’t create tasks unless you add a task block.

questions.question: What advantage does embedding a Figma file directly into a wiki page provide?

options.text: It converts the design into a PDF that can be downloaded.
options.explanation: Embedding shows the live file; it doesn’t convert it.

options.text: Team members can view and jump to design concepts without leaving Notion.
options.explanation: Embedding lets users see and interact with the design in‑context, streamlining workflow.

options.text: It automatically syncs design changes to the product codebase.
options.explanation: Embedding is a visual link; it doesn’t push changes to code.

options.text: It locks the design file so only PMs can edit it.
options.explanation: Permissions are managed in Figma, not by embedding.

questions.question: If a product manager wants to see only the research sessions conducted by a specific teammate, which Notion feature should they use?

options.text: Create a new page and manually copy those rows.
options.explanation: Manual copying defeats the purpose of dynamic filtering.

options.text: Add a filter to the table view based on the "Interviewer" property.
options.explanation: Filters let you display a subset of rows, such as sessions by a particular person.

options.text: Change the database type from Table to List.
options.explanation: Changing view type doesn’t filter data.

options.text: Use the "Sort" feature to order rows alphabetically by name.
options.explanation: Sorting rearranges rows but doesn’t limit which rows are shown.

questions.question: Which statement best captures the relationship between a product wiki and onboarding new team members, as described in the article?

options.text: New hires must read the wiki once and then rely on senior staff for all questions.
options.explanation: The article emphasizes the wiki as a living resource, reducing reliance on constant guidance.

options.text: The wiki provides a centralized, searchable knowledge base that new members can consult repeatedly, turning onboarding into an ongoing conversation.
options.explanation: This reflects the guide’s point that onboarding becomes a two‑way, continuous process via the wiki.

options.text: Onboarding is handled entirely outside of Notion, using separate HR software.
options.explanation: While other tools may exist, the article positions the wiki as the central onboarding hub.

options.text: The wiki replaces all face‑to‑face training sessions.
options.explanation: The wiki complements, not replaces, personal training.
